fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that HTTP test double toolkit we've been talking about. The idea is that devs can spin up fake request/response objects in their handler tests without needing a real server running. Think of it like what we did for the login module's compatibility shims, but for the full HTTP layer.

On the request side, people need to be able to set things like headers, route params, body, cookies, and so on. Header reads should work regardless of how the dev capitalizes things — we got a bug report last sprint where someone's tests kept failing because of casing inconsistencies. Also the referrer thing keeps coming up (you know, the two spellings), make sure that's handled. There's also some content negotiation stuff — Accept headers, encoding preferences, language, charset — all that should resolve against a list of candidates the dev provides.

Range parsing is needed too; if the range is outside the file size it should give back some kind of negative signal rather than throwing, and absent ranges should be distinguishable from bad ones.

On the response side, cookies need to support a 'clear' operation that sets an expiry in the past. Also need redirect, render, JSON/JSONP send, write/end chunking, header management (append, remove, vary de-dup), and a format-negotiation helper that falls back to 406 if nothing matches.

Parameter resolution should check route params first, then body, then query — and falsy values like 0 should not be skipped over.

trigger_keywords: what fields does a new request have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A newly created request defaults to method GET, empty string for url/original_url/base_url/path, empty objects for params/cookies/headers/body/query/files, and absent (undefined/null) for session and signed_cookies.

Header lookup must be case-insensitive. Both the direct lookup method (e.g., req.get()) and any aliased method must return the stored value regardless of the casing used during lookup or storage. Missing headers must return an absent value (e.g., undefined), not throw.
The header lookup must treat 'referer' and 'referrer' as interchangeable aliases. Looking up either spelling should return the value stored under either spelling.

The request object must support four negotiation operations: body_type (matches against content-type header), accept (matches against Accept header), encoding (matches against Accept-Encoding), charset (matches against Accept-Charset), and language (matches against Accept-Language). Each takes a list of candidates and returns the first match, or boolean false if none match.

trigger_keywords: range absent vs invalid vs unsatisfiable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Range parsing has three distinct outcomes: (1) absent — no Range header present, return undefined/null; (2) -1 — range is unsatisfiable given the resource size (e.g., start offset beyond file size); (3) -2 — range header is malformed/unparseable. Valid ranges return an object with a type and an array of inclusive {start, end} objects. An optional combine flag collapses adjacent/overlapping ranges.

trigger_keywords: route param vs body vs query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parameter resolution checks route params (req.params) first, then body fields (req.body), then query string fields (req.query). Falsy values such as 0 or empty string that are explicitly stored must be returned as real values, not skipped. A caller-supplied default is only returned when the key is genuinely absent from all three sources.

The mock request must be able to emit its body payload as stream events: a 'data' event carrying the body bytes followed by an 'end' event. String payloads emit as-is; structured objects are serialized to JSON strings; Buffer payloads emit raw bytes. The end signal must always follow the data event.

The hostname is derived from the Host header by stripping the port number. Subdomains are returned as an array ordered from nearest to farthest (e.g., 'a.b.example.com' → ['a', 'b']). An explicitly provided hostname option overrides the Host header derivation.

A newly created response starts with status code 200, an empty cookies map, an empty headers map, empty data and buffer strings, absent encoding, ended=false, headers_sent=false, writable_finished=false, and any supplied locals object exposed directly.

Clearing a cookie (clearCookie) must write an empty string value with the expiration date set to the earliest positive epoch millisecond (i.e., new Date(1) or equivalent) and path set to '/'. Any other caller-supplied options (e.g., domain, httpOnly) must be preserved; only the expires and path fields are forcibly overridden.

Calling sendStatus(code) must: set the status code, set the Content-Type header to 'text/plain', set the body data to the standard HTTP status message string for that code (e.g., 404 → 'Not Found'), and mark the response as ended (ended=true, headers_sent=true, writable_finished=true).

res.json() must set Content-Type to 'application/json'. res.jsonp() must set Content-Type to 'text/javascript'. Both operations serialize the value to a JSON string and store it as body data, and both mark the response as ended.
The Vary header management must deduplicate values — adding the same field name to Vary more than once must not produce duplicate entries. The comparison should be case-insensitive.
redirect(url) uses status 302 by default. redirect(status, url) uses the supplied status code. Both forms must emit 'end' and 'finish' events and record the redirect URL for inspection.

res.format() accepts a map of media-type keys to handler functions and invokes the first handler whose key matches the request's Accept header. If no key matches and no 'default' key is provided, the response must be set to status 406 (Not Acceptable).

res.write(chunk, encoding?) appends string chunks to a string data accumulator and Buffer chunks to a separate buffer accumulator. The encoding of the first write call is recorded. headers_sent becomes true on the first write. res.end(chunk?) appends a final chunk (if supplied) and then sets ended=true and writable_finished=true.

trigger_keywords: when path is supplied instead of url
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a path option is supplied to createRequest() but no url option is provided, the path value becomes the observable url field on the request object.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The case-insensitive header lookup and referrer/referer alias logic described in feature1_2_request_header_lookup.json, implemented in the MockRequest header get() method
Range parsing sentinel values: -1 for unsatisfiable ranges (start > size-1) and -2 for malformed/unparseable Range header strings, as specified in feature1_4_request_range_parsing.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: clear operation that sets an expiry in the past
context_pointers.actual_meaning: res.clearCookie() sets expires to new Date(1) (earliest positive epoch millisecond) and path to '/', as specified in feature2_1_response_initial_state_and_cookies.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: falsy values like 0 should not be skipped over
context_pointers.actual_meaning: In req.param(name, default), the resolution logic must use explicit undefined/null checks rather than truthiness checks, so that stored values of 0, false, or empty string are returned rather than falling through to body/query or the default, per feature1_5_request_parameter_resolution.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full clarity on right now — I'd recommend checking the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD sections in start.md for the canonical behavior. If you can point me to the specific scenario you're unsure about I can try to clarify from a product perspective.

feature1_1_request_defaults_and_options..description: A request object exposes default HTTP request state and copies supplied route, URL, header, body, query, file, cookie, and custom metadata into observable fields.

feature1_2_request_header_lookup.json.description: Request header lookup is case-insensitive and treats the common referrer spelling variants as equivalent; missing headers produce an absent value.

cases.expected_output:
lookup_KEY1=value1
alias_KEY1=value1
lookup_key2=value2
alias_key2=value2
lookup_missing=absent
alias_missing=absent

feature1_3_request_content_negotiation.j.description: Request content negotiation reports the first acceptable media type, encoding, charset, or language from a candidate list and returns false when none match.

cases.expected_output:
match_0=html
match_1=false
match_2=html
match_3=gzip
match_4=utf-8
match_5=en-GB

feature1_4_request_range_parsing.json.description: A request range parser returns an absent result for absent ranges, negative sentinel values for invalid or unsatisfiable ranges, and inclusive byte ranges for valid headers with optional combining.

cases.expected_output: range=absent
range=-1

feature1_5_request_parameter_resolution..description: Request parameter lookup checks route parameters before body fields before query fields, preserves falsy values, and returns a caller-provided fallback when absent.

cases.expected_output: value=from-route
value=0

feature1_6_request_stream_and_host_helpe.description: A mock request can emit supplied body payloads as stream data and derives hostname and subdomain information from the host header unless an explicit hostname is provided.

cases.expected_output:
data=test data
ended=true

data={"key":"value"}
ended=true

feature2_1_response_initial_state_and_co.description: A response object starts with success status, empty cookies, and supplied local variables; cookie operations store values with options and clearing a cookie writes an expired empty cookie.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
status_message=OK
headers={}
cookies={"name":{"options":absent,"value":"value"}}
data=
buffer=
encoding=absent
ended=false
headers_sent=false
writable_finished=false
data_length_valid=true

feature2_2_response_status_and_body_seri.description: Response status and body operations update status code, content type, serialized data, encoding, completion flags, and emitted body data for text, status messages, JSON values, and JSONP values.

cases.expected_output:
status=404
status_message=OK
headers={}
cookies={}
data=
buffer=
encoding=absent
ended=false

data_length_valid=true

cookies={}
data=Not Found
buffer=
encoding=absent
ended=true
headers_sent=true
writable_finished=true
data_length_valid=true

status=200
status_message=OK
headers={}
cookies={}
data=payload
buffer=
encoding=absent
ended=true
headers_sent=true
writable_finished=true
data_length_valid=true

feature2_3_response_header_management.js.description: Response header management supports MIME type resolution, direct and bulk setting, appending repeated values, Vary de-duplication, case-insensitive reads, names listing, presence checks, removal, and location headers.

feature2_4_response_write_end_and_head.j.description: Low-level response writing concatenates string and buffer payloads, tracks encoding and finish state, writes status line and headers before body transmission, and preserves already-sent headers from later status writes.
